text: The video shows the repair of a boctelic hernia in an adult using the da Vinci robotic system. The surgeon operates with a standard two-arm technique and a high-definition camera.
The surgeon identifies a small posterior lateral hernia with an intact hernia sac and uses hook monopolar cautery to dissect it from the surrounding diaphragm tissue.

The surgeon excises the bulk of the properitoneal fat from the hernia to reduce its mass and prevent incarceration of hernia sac contents. The excised fat is retrieved with an endo-catch bag and passed off the field through the assistance port.

The surgeon achieves clear visualization of the hernia defect edges, facilitating the reduction of the remaining hernia sac contents into the abdominal cavity. The diaphragm is dissected to create a clean edge for the upcoming repair.
The surgeon fashions a GORE-TEX patch using dual mesh for the defect repair, securing it laterally to the thoracic wall with two Ethibond sutures and a tie knot system.

The surgeon uses a running non-absorbable suture to secure the mesh circumferentially through the full thickness of the diaphragmatic defect, effectively recreating the separation between the abdominal and thoracic cavities. The redundant patch material is trimmed with endoshears to ensure a tight fit without laxity in the diaphragmatic repair.

The surgeon initiates a second stitch posteriorly, bringing it around to meet the anterior portion of the hernia repair. The lateral wall, previously anchored with the tie knot system, is reinforced with a third running non-absorbable suture.
